retrieved_docs.title: Tumor-Suppressor Genes -- Clinical Significance -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: Phosphatase and Tensin Homolog ( PTEN ) Gene: PTEN gene encodes a lipid phosphatase that negatively regulates the phosphoinositide-3-kinase (PI3K)-AKT and the target of mTOR signaling pathways. These pathways are vital for cell proliferation, cell cycle progression, and apoptosis. [24] The PTEN protein also functions to keep migration, adhesion, and angiogenesis in check. It also plays a role in the overall stabilization of the genome. A biallelic loss-of-function is common in diverse cancers. Cowden syndrome is an autosomal dominant disorder resulting from germline loss-of-function mutations of this gene and correlates with a higher risk of breast and endometrial cancer

retrieved_docs.title: Tumor-Suppressor Genes -- Clinical Significance -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: Many tumor suppressor genes have been the object of studies, and there are likely many more that have yet to be discovered. The mechanisms of each tumor suppressor gene and its protein products are complex and interrelated to other cell signaling pathways, but some better-known mechanisms appear below.

retrieved_docs.title: Tumor-Suppressor Genes -- Review Questions
retrieved_docs.content: Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Comment on this article.

retrieved_docs.title: Tumor-Suppressor Genes -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Classic tumor suppressor genes (TSGs) are recessive at the cellular level, with inactivation of both alleles typically found in tumors. Inheritance of a solitary mutant allele increases tumor susceptibility because only a single additional inactivating event is necessary for complete loss of gene function. The same gene is often inactivated in sporadic cancers. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Tumor-Suppressor Genes -- Clinical Significance -- Points to Remember
retrieved_docs.content: Tumor suppressor genes function to either repress or inhibit the cell cycle or promote apoptosis. The more specific functions of tumor suppressor proteins fall into several categories, including [1] [21] : Inhibition of mitogenic signaling pathways Inhibition of cell cycle progression Inhibition of “pro-growth” programs of metabolism and angiogenesis Inhibition of invasion and metastasis Stabilization of the genome DNA repair factors Induction of apoptosis

retrieved_docs.title: Cowden Disease -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The protein encoded by the PTEN gene contributes to the control of apoptosis and the cell cycle. [12] Specifically, the phosphatidylinositol 3-kinase (PI3K)/AKT/mammalian target of rapamycin (mTOR) pathway is down-regulated by the PTEN gene product, resulting in decreased cellular proliferation and survival. [1] When heterozygosity for the PTEN gene is lost by a "second hit" mutation, the resulting phenotype of multiple hamartomas and neoplasms is produced. [13] Most commonly, patients experience macrocephaly along with mucocutaneous lesions such as oral papillomas, tricholemmomas, and acral keratoses.

retrieved_docs.title: Peutz-Jeghers Syndrome -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Individuals with PJS have an increased risk of developing malignant gastrointestinal tumors, including colorectal, stomach, and pancreatic cancers. Neoplasms in nongastrointestinal sites, such as the breasts, ovaries, and uterus, are also common. The tumor-suppressive role of STK11 is crucial for protecting against these malignancies, and mutations in STK11 increase the likelihood of 2nd-hit mutations in other oncogenes and tumor suppressor genes, including KRAS , TP53 , and PTEN . These genetic alterations drive carcinogenesis, though the sequence of events varies between individuals and tumor types.

retrieved_docs.title: Tubular Adenoma -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Cowden syndrome and PTEN hamartoma: PTEN acts as a tumor suppressor gene. Cowden syndrome, as an autosomal dominant syndrome and related to PTEN hamartoma tumor syndrome (PHTS), has a broad range of clinical manifestations, including trichilemmomas in the face and malignant pathologies in the breast, thyroid, and gastrointestinal polyps. Obtaining timely screening schedules to exclude malignancies in these patients is crucial. Traditionally, there was a remarkable 80% germline PTEN mutation in patients affected by Cowden syndrome; however, recently, the specificity of these criteria has been questioned. [23]

retrieved_docs.title: Cardiac Cancer -- Etiology -- Cowden syndrome
retrieved_docs.content: Cowden syndrome (eg, phosphatase and tensin homolog [PTEN] hamartoma tumor syndrome) is an autosomal dominant condition due to PTEN mutations, characterized by multiple hamartomas and a risk of malignancies (eg, breast and thyroid). Cardiac lipomas have been noted in Cowden syndrome, consistent with the propensity for benign overgrowths. Thus, an incidental cardiac lipoma, especially if multifocal, might warrant consideration of an underlying PTEN mutation. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Low-Grade Gliomas -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: LGGs grow slowly and can be followed over the years without treatment unless they cause symptoms and grow. Multiple acquired genetic mutations are found in gliomas. Tumor suppressor protein 53 (p53), phosphatase and tensin homolog (PTEN), and epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) are involved in the pathogenesis of these tumors. p53 is the "guardian of the genome" and ensures the DNA is copied correctly; it destroys the cell if it is mutated. p53 usually mutates early in the disease, and other mutations can survive. EGFR normally stimulates cells to divide, but if it gets amplified, it stimulates cells to divide too much. Together these mutations cause uncontrollable cell division, a hallmark of cancer. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Trichilemmoma -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Cowden syndrome is an autosomal dominant disease caused by a mutation in the phosphatase and tensin homolog ( PTEN ) gene, which is a tumor suppressor gene. This syndrome is part of a group of associated disorders caused by germline mutations in the PTEN gene, which includes Bannayan-Riley-Ruvalcaba syndrome, Proteus, and Proteus-like syndrome. [12] Cowden syndrome is a rare condition and has an estimated prevalence of 1 in 200,000 live births. This syndrome is characterized by numerous hamartomas and an increased risk of several malignancies, including breast, thyroid, endometrial, renal, colorectal carcinoma, and melanoma. [17] Additionally, individuals with Cowden syndrome may exhibit other associated features such as intestinal polyposis, macrocephaly, scoliosis, and intellectual disability. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Premalignant Lesions of the Endometrium -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The tumor progression model suggests that loss of PTEN occurs in sporadic glands in the normal endometrium, but this loss is insufficient to drive atypical glandular proliferation and progression. The PTEN negative glands need to acquire additional molecular ‘hits’ which contribute to the formation of AH/EIN. [7] The loss of PTEN function causes the PI3K/AKT pathway to become overactive. There is evidence that PI3K/AKT signaling enhances or turns on estrogen receptor-targeted gene expression. Therefore, loss of PTEN function may stimulate estrogen-dependent gene expression, leading to the overgrowth of cell types that depend on estrogen for trophic signals, such as endometrial and mammary epithelial cells. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Tumor-Suppressor Genes -- Clinical Significance -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: CDKN2A: This complex encodes two tumor suppressor proteins, p16/INK4a, and ARF, which augments RB function and stabilizes p53, respectively. [34] Loss-of-function germline mutations in this gene occur in autosomal dominant familial melanoma. A biallelic loss-of-function presents in multiple cancers including melanomas, leukemias, and carcinomas.

retrieved_docs.title: Renal Clear Cell Cancer -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: While most of the cases of clear cell carcinoma are sporadic, 2 to 3% of the cases are related to genetic alterations in the von Hippel landau gene, chromatin remodeling genes/epigenetic regulators (Polybromo 1, BRCA protein 1, and SET D1), and genetic alterations in pathways that disrupt signal transduction involving the PI3K/Akt/mTOR pathway. [16] [17] PTEN deficiency is usually associated with a more aggressive phenotype. [18] While angiomyolipoma (AML) is the most common renal neoplasm seen in patients with tuberous sclerosis, clear cell tumors may be seen rarely. [19] Clear cell tumors can also be seen in a small minority of patients with Birt Hogg Dube syndrome, who have been otherwise shown to develop hybrid-chromophobe tumors and oncocytomas. [20] [21]

retrieved_docs.title: Granular Cell Tumor -- Etiology -- Syndrome Associations
retrieved_docs.content: It is known that the clinical finding of multiple granular cell tumors is associated with Noonan syndrome, neurofibromatosis I, and LEOPARD syndrome. [15] [16] [17] [18] [19] [20] Authors have reported PTPN11 gene mutations in granular cell tumors associated with LEOPARD and Noonan syndromes. [15] It has also been proposed that the etiology of multiple granular cell tumors may be linked to abnormal RAS/MAPK pathway cell signaling, given that this mutation is a shared feature in all 3 syndromes. In another study, granular cell tumor was also associated with germline PTEN mutations in patients with PTEN hamartoma tumor syndrome. [21]

retrieved_docs.title: A Review of Hereditary Colorectal Cancers -- Etiology -- High-Penetrance Genetic Syndromes
retrieved_docs.content: Individuals with PTEN mutations face a 2- to 3-fold increased lifetime risk of CRC, along with an elevated risk of breast, thyroid, and renal cancers. These mutations may also contribute to a higher likelihood of endometrial cancer and melanoma. [45] Additionally, PTEN mutations have been linked to various benign conditions, including macrocephaly, Hashimoto's thyroiditis, mucocutaneous hamartomas, vascular malformations, and neurodevelopmental disorders such as autism. [46] An increased risk of immunological dysregulation, including autoimmunity and immune deficiencies, [43] has also been associated with these mutations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 1 -- Community 257
retrieved_docs.content: importance of assessing metastatic potential even when the primary lesion’s benign versus malignant nature is unclear. Collectively, these interrelated entities form a hierarchical and functional network that connects genetic syndromes, primary gastrointestinal cancers, various metastatic malignancies, and skin melanomas, providing essential context for accurate diagnosis, staging, and management in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 4 -- Community 2918
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant tumor cells represent the abnormal cellular component that defines and drives the disease known as السرطان البنكرياسي (pancreatic cancer), indicating that the presence of these atypical, cancerous cells is the hallmark of this specific malignancy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2701
retrieved_docs.content: Primary malignant neoplasm of the head of the pancreas, primary malignant neoplasm of the nasal bone, and primary malignant neoplasm of the thymus are each examples of primary malignant neoplastic proliferations, which constitute pathological processes. These entities represent malignant tumor growth arising in distinct anatomical sites—the pancreatic head, the nasal bone, and the thymus—characterized by uncontrolled proliferation of cancerous cells. As pathological processes, they require oncologic assessment, appropriate staging, and targeted therapeutic strategies to manage the aggressive nature of the malignancy in each location.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2556
retrieved_docs.content: Primary malignant neoplasm of the nasal bone and primary malignant neoplasm of the thymus are both examples of primary malignant neoplastic proliferations, representing pathological processes in which cancerous cells originate and grow aggressively at their respective anatomical sites. As primary malignant neoplastic proliferations, each condition reflects a direct, localized malignant transformation of tissue—one affecting the nasal bone and the other affecting the thymus. Recognizing that both entities share the same fundamental nature as pathological processes of primary malignant neoplastic growth aids clinicians in understanding their aggressive behavior, guiding diagnostic evaluation, and informing treatment strategies tailored to the specific organ involved.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 1 -- Community 240
retrieved_docs.content: similarly defined as a primary malignant neoplastic proliferation. In addition, an inverse‑is‑a relationship links the entity “سرطانة توتية” to the primary malignant tumor morphology (“ورم خبيث أولي”), further emphasizing its classification as a type of primary malignant neoplasm. Together, these relationships illustrate how various primary malignant neoplasms across different organs are unified by common pathological processes, shared morphological descriptors, and hierarchical links to broader cancer concepts, aiding in consistent classification and clinical understanding.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1908
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant tumor cells are the abnormal cells that define the disease known as سرطانة العظم (bone cancer).

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 1039
retrieved_docs.content: Primary malignant neoplasm of the head of the pancreas and primary malignant neoplasm of the thymus are both classified as primary malignant neoplastic proliferations, which are pathological processes. As malignant tumors arising in distinct anatomical sites—the pancreatic head and the thymus—each represents an uncontrolled growth of neoplastic cells that characterizes a serious pathological condition. Recognizing these neoplasms as primary malignant neoplastic proliferations emphasizes their role as aggressive cancerous processes that require prompt diagnostic evaluation and targeted oncologic management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 4 -- Community 2902
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant tumor cells are abnormal cells that characterize cancers classified as other and unspecified primary, indicating that these atypical cells constitute the pathological basis of such unspecified primary malignancies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 3 -- Community 2623
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic Lesion is recognized as a diagnostic finding associated with سرطانة العظم, indicating that the disease has spread beyond its primary site. In the context of سرطانة العظم, Tumor cells, malignant are identified as the abnormal cellular component that drives the malignancy. Together, the presence of malignant tumor cells and the detection of metastatic lesions provide critical information for diagnosing, staging, and managing سرطانة العظم, guiding therapeutic decisions and prognostic assessment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 247
retrieved_docs.content: specified sites, malignant skin cancer (سرطان الجلد الخبيث), melanomas of skin (a subtype of malignant skin cancer that also encompasses malignant lip cancer), prostate adenocarcinoma, squamous‑cell carcinoma of the head and neck, lung carcinoma of non‑smokers, and generic metastatic cancer. Melanomas of skin have been reported with a finding site in the retina, highlighting possible ocular involvement. Collectively, these hierarchical and inverse‑is‑a relationships create an integrated network that connects hereditary syndromes, primary organ‑specific cancers, and diverse metastatic presentations to the central concept of malignant tumors and secondary malignancies, supporting precise diagnosis, classification, and management of oncologic patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 4 -- Community 2901
retrieved_docs.content: Benign tumor cells are not considered a finding associated with ovarian cancer.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 3 -- Community 2372
retrieved_docs.content: cancer; other and unspecified primary and سرطان الغدة الدرقية are both classified as subtypes of ورم صماوي خبيث, which denotes a malignant tumor. This relationship indicates that these specific cancers fall under the broader category of malignant tumors, sharing the characteristic of uncontrolled, invasive cell growth. Recognizing them as forms of ورم صماوي خبيث helps guide diagnostic evaluation, treatment planning, and prognostic assessment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 4 -- Community 2884
retrieved_docs.content: Tumor cells, uncertain whether benign or malignant, are identified as abnormal cells of disease and are classified under the category “cancer; other and unspecified primary,” indicating that these cells represent a form of cancer for which the primary site cannot be determined. This classification underscores the diagnostic uncertainty surrounding such tumor cells and highlights the need for further pathological evaluation to clarify their nature and guide appropriate patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 4 -- Community 2888
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant Cellular Infiltrate is recognized as a diagnostic finding of cancer of the ovary, indicating the presence of abnormal tissue within the ovarian region. Similarly, Tumor cells, malignant are identified as the abnormal cellular component of cancer of the ovary, representing the malignant cells that drive the disease process. Together, these pathological entities underscore the cellular abnormalities that characterize ovarian cancer and are essential for its diagnosis and understanding.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 4 -- Community 2734
retrieved_docs.content: Female Reproductive System Disorder is a broad clinical category that includes a range of conditions affecting the female reproductive organs. Within this category, cancer of the uterus represents a malignant neoplasm of the uterine tissue, while ورم مهبلي حميد (benign vaginal tumor) denotes a non‑malignant growth in the vagina. Recognizing both uterine cancer and benign vaginal tumors as specific manifestations of Female Reproductive System Disorder highlights their shared relevance to gynecologic health and underscores the importance of tailored diagnostic and therapeutic strategies for each condition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2581
retrieved_docs.content: Tumor cells, malignant and tumor cells, uncertain whether benign or malignant are both classified as abnormal cells of disease and are linked to cancer; other and unspecified primary, with a specific association to pancreatic cancer (السرطان البنكرياسي).
